m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the following code segment doing ?
void fn( )
{
char c;
cin.get(c);
  if (c != ‘ ’)
  {
   fn( );
   cout.put(c);
  }
}

Options.OptionText: The string entered is printed as it is.
The string entered is printed in reverse order.
It will go in an infinite loop.
It will print an empty line.

m_Item1.Explanation: fn is recursive function so entered string is printed in reverse order.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A mathematical-model with a collection of operations defined on that model is called
m_Item1.Explanation: In computer science, an abstract data type (ADT) is a mathematical model for data types, where a data type is defined by its behavior (semantics) from the point of view of a user of the data, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The complexity of multiplying two matrices of order m*n and n*p is
m_Item1.Explanation: In matrix multiplication  we are using 3 loops since execution of each for loop requires time complexity , So for three loops it becomes mnp

m_Item1.QuestionText: The minimum number of multiplications and additions/subtraction required to evaluate the polynomial P = 4x3+3x2 -15x+45 is
m_Item1.Explanation: Three multiplication for 4X3, 3X2,,15X and addition , subtractions are also 3

m_Item1.QuestionText: What data structure would you mostly likely see in a nonrecursive implementation of a recursive algorithm ?
m_Item1.Explanation: In recursive algorithms, the order in which the recursive process comes back is the reverse of the order in which it goes forward during execution. The compiler uses the stack data structure to implement recursion. In the forwarding phase, the values of local variables, parameters and the return address are pushed into the stack at each recursion level. In the backing-out phase, the stacked address is popped and used to execute the rest of the code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: O<sub>(N)</sub> (linear time) is better than O<sub>(1)</sub> constant time.
m_Item1.Explanation: O(1) is constant-time, which means that the operation does not depend up on the input size, and O(n) is linear time, which means that the operation changes linearly with input size.

m_Item1.QuestionText: An ADT is defined to be a mathematical model of a user-defined type along with the collection of all ____________ operations on that model.
m_Item1.Explanation: In computer science, an abstract data type (ADT) is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its behavior (semantics) from the point of view of a user of the data, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following case does not exist in complexity theory
m_Item1.Explanation: complexity can be measured in terms of best, average or worst case, & NULL can not be the case

m_Item1.QuestionText:
How can I dynamically allocate a two-dimensional array?
   1) int **array1 = (int **)malloc(nrows * sizeof(int *));
   for(i = 0; i < nrows; i++)
   array1[i] = (int *)malloc(ncolumns * sizeof(int));

   2) int **array2 = (int **)malloc(nrows * sizeof(int *));
   array2[0] = (int *)malloc(nrows * ncolumns * sizeof(int));
   for(i = 1; i < nrows; i++)
   array2[i] = array2[0] + i * ncolumns;

   3) int *array3 = (int *)malloc(nrows * ncolumns * sizeof(int));

   4) int (array4)[ncolumns]=malloc(nrows*sizeof(*array4))
m_Item1.Explanation:
In first 2 ways memory is getting allocated firstly for rows & then for every columns in rows.
In 3rd way memory is getting allocated for (row*col) these many blocks . so all are correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
int size=5;
void main( )
{
int Array[size+1];
printf("%d", sizeof(Array) );
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Array Size must be constant. Here "size" is integer variable so not allowed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following data structure is not linear data structure?
m_Item1.Explanation: Array & Linked list both are linear data structures.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program ?
#include<stdio.h>
int main()
{
float arr[] = {12.4, 2.3, 4.5, 6.7};
printf('%d ', sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]));
return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: arr is array of 4 float variable so size of arr is 16 bytes , and sizeof(arr[0]) is size for one varibale which is 4 bytes. So expression is 16/4 and asnwer is 4.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Arrays are best data structures

Options.OptionText: for relatively permanent collections of data
for the size of the structure and the data in the structure are constantly changing

m_Item1.Explanation: Array is collection of relative data members, that can be changed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Each array declaration need not give, implicitly or explicitly, the information about

Options.OptionText: the first data from the set to be stored
the index set of the array

m_Item1.Explanation: While declaring array datatype, array name , size of array(index set) are required, first element is not required.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The elements of an array are stored successively in memory cells because

Options.OptionText: by this way computer can keep track of only the address of the first element and the addresses of other elements can be calculated
the architecture of computer memory does not allow arrays to store other than serially

m_Item1.Explanation: By knowing base address of array element , next successive elements address & values can be calculated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The organization and management of data structures take place in:
m_Item1.Explanation: Current program data members will be organised or created on primary/main memory which is used by current program.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Point out the correct statement that will let you access the elements of the array using 'p' in the following program?
#include<stdio.h>
#include<stdlib.h>
int main()
{
    int i, j;
    int(*p)[3];
    p = (int(*)[3])malloc(3*sizeof(*p));
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText:
for(i=0; i<3; i++)
{
for(j=0; j<3; j++)
printf("%d", p[i+j]);
}

for(i=0; i<3; i++)
{
for(j=0; j<3; j++)
printf("%d", p[i][j]);
}

m_Item1.Explanation: In this p is array of 3 integer pointers, & pointer can be a address of  internal array(internal array of columns), so will be treated as 2 d array as shown(i.e 1* & 1[])

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following Data Structure is/are linear Data Structures?
m_Item1.Explanation: string is also array of char, list & queue are also list/array, so all are linear.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which data structure allows deleting data elements from front and inserting at rear?
m_Item1.Explanation: In queue, we always dequeue (or access) data, pointed by front pointer and while enqueing (or storing) data in the queue we take help of rear pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Finding the location of the element with a given value is:
m_Item1.Explanation: Searching is use to find value at given location.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Identify the data structure which allows deletions at both ends of the list but insertion at only one end.
m_Item1.Explanation: In input restricted double ended queue, insertion is permitted on only 1 end but deletion can be done from both(front & rear) end.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When new data are to be inserted into a data structure, but there is no available space; this situation is usually called
m_Item1.Explanation: When any data structure exceed the size limit,. This situaltion is Overflow.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program in Turbo-C ?
#include<stdio.h>
   int main()
   {
      int arr[5], i=-1, z;
      while(i<5)
      arr[i]=++i;
      for(i=0; i<5; i++)
      printf('%d, ', arr[i]);
   return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: initial value of i is -1 and In while we are checking  i <5, so ++i will increment  first i.e 0 & then will be stored in array  so on index 0 value is 0, & so on up to 4 it is 4.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;

void main()
{
char p[]="This is a test";
cout<<sizeof(p)<<","<<strlen(p);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Sizeof array is actual number of charecters plus one that is for  '' so sizeof(p) is 15.  And string length is 14 which is actual number of character .

m_Item1.QuestionText: Term Data Structure refers to _________ and interrelationship between them
m_Item1.Explanation: Datastructure is organization & retrival of data in a efficient way.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following data structure is non-linear type?
m_Item1.Explanation: String is nothing but character array. And array,linklist,stack,queue these are linear data structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText: An algorithm that calls itself directly or indirectly is known as
m_Item1.Explanation: The process in which a function calls itself directly or indirectly is called recursion and the corresponding function is called as recursive function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In linear data structure each and every element can/may have ____ predecessor and _____ successor.
m_Item1.Explanation: In linear data structure every element is  stored on next successive address, 1 element after every element & it will be unique.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The data structure required to check (in Infix expression)  whether an expression contains balanced parenthesis or not?
m_Item1.Explanation: The stack is a simple data structure in which elements are added and removed based on the LIFO principle. Open parenthesis is pushed into the stack and a closed parenthesis pops out elements till the top element of the stack is its corresponding open parenthesis. If the stack is empty, parenthesis is balanced otherwise it is unbalanced.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In _________ data structure , data contain hierarchical and network relationship between elements.
m_Item1.Explanation: Non linear data structures contain data in either hierarchical/network type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Two main measures for the efficiency of an algorithm are
m_Item1.Explanation: Time complexity of an algorithm quantifies the amount of time taken by an algorithm to run as a function of the length of the input. Similarly, Space complexity of an algorithm quantifies the amount of space or memory taken by an algorithm to run as a function of the length of the input

m_Item1.QuestionText: The space factor when determining the efficiency of algorithm is measured by

Options.OptionText: Counting the maximum memory needed by the algorithm
Counting the minimum memory needed by the algorithm
Counting the average memory needed by the algorithm
Counting the maximum disk space needed by the algorithm

m_Item1.Explanation: Space complexity is a measure of the amount of working storage an algorithm needs. That means how much memory, in the worst case, is needed at any point in the algorithm.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What does the following piece of code do?
#define ROW 3
#define COL 3
for(int i = 0; i < ROW; i++)
{ 
    for(int j = 0; j < column; j++)
    {
        if(i == j)
            sum = sum + (array[i][j]);
    }
}
printf(“%d”,sum);
m_Item1.Explanation: Here trace of the matrix is the sum of the elements of the main diagonal i.e the diagonal from the upper left to the lower right of a matrix. Normal of the matrix is the square root of the sum of all the elements.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following  is true about characteristics of abstract data type

I.       It exports a Type

II.       It Exports a set of operations
m_Item1.Explanation: ADT is having it's own data members and it's own fucntion, set of rules . So both statements are true.
